title: Lab Report Brackets
statement_md:
Your physics lab partner has scribbled the final formula for today's experiment, and it is packed with brackets — round `()`, square `[]`, and curly `{}`. Before you copy it into the record notebook (in pen — no whitener allowed), you want to check just the bracket structure.

The rule is simple: every opening bracket must be closed by a bracket of the **same type**, and brackets must close in the right order — the one opened most recently closes first. So `([]{})` is fine, but `([)]` would earn red ink from the lab in-charge.

You are given the bracket sequences from `T` formulas (everything else already stripped out). For each one, decide whether the brackets are properly matched and nested.
input_format_md:
The first line contains a single integer `T` — the number of formulas to check.

Each of the next `T` lines contains one non-empty string `S` made only of the characters `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`, `{`, `}`.
output_format_md: For each test case, print `YES` if the bracket sequence is properly matched and nested, otherwise print `NO`, each on its own line.
hints: Read the formula left to right. Every opening bracket is a promise that must be paid back later — and the most recent promise gets paid first. Which data structure remembers things in most-recent-first order?

Use a stack: push every opener. On a closer, the stack top must be the matching opener — pop it; if the stack is empty or the top doesn't match, it's `NO`. After the last character, the stack must be empty for `YES`.
